ui.title: CoZe
ui.welcomeSub:
Classification and aptitude test
pursuant to §365 of the AI Operational Safety Regulation
on the commercial operation of Large Language Models.
ui.infoPoints: Scoring: Each question carries 2 to 5 penalty points
ui.confirmAbort: Do you want to cancel the exam? Your progress will be lost.
ui.timeExpiredAlert: Exam time expired! The exam will be submitted automatically.
ui.mediaDescDefault: Situation image for the current exam question.
ui.noImageDesc: No image is required for this question. Answer based on the text description.
ui.submitModalText: Do you really want to finish and submit the theoretical exam? Unanswered questions will be scored as incorrect.
ui.resultDisclaimer: Unofficial practice result. Not affiliated with Microsoft Corporation or the creator's employers.
ui.legalSec1Text: Completing this "exam" does not confer any officially recognized qualification, certificate, or proof of competence. It is an unofficial training and humor tool.
ui.legalSec2Text: Microsoft, Microsoft 365, M365 Copilot, Teams, SharePoint, Outlook, Purview, and other named product designations are registered trademarks of Microsoft Corporation in the USA and/or other countries. This application is not supported, sponsored, operated, or maintained by Microsoft.
ui.legalSec3Text: This quiz is a private leisure and community project of the creator. It does not represent the views, policies, training materials, or official guidance of current or former employers.
ui.legalSec4Text: References such as "§365 AI Operational Safety Regulation" are purely fictional devices parodying classic driving-school theory exams.

questions.text: A business user connects an unstructured SharePoint folder with 6,000 legacy documents as the knowledge base for their Copilot agent. The answers are irrelevant and contradictory. They complain about the quality. How do you respond?
questions.pictureDesc: Scenario: Data flood! A massive unstructured folder blocks the Copilot agent's groundedness.

answers.text: Advise the user to connect only a curated set of relevant documents, not the entire folder wholesale.
Floor it in a hazard acceleration to escape the data flood.
Advise the user to dump another 6,000 unstructured files in. More data famously improves answer quality.
Promise the user that Copilot will support this "soon," and end the call quickly.

questions.text: A client demands connecting a 20-year-old on-premises database with no REST API to Microsoft 365 Copilot. What do you need to watch for?
questions.pictureDesc: Scenario: An obsolete on-prem database is clogging the modern data highway of M365 Copilot.

answers.text: The project needs a Microsoft Graph connector agent (or on-premises gateway) to get the data into the Graph index.
Manually type the data into Excel while driving.
Ignore the obstacle. When in doubt, Copilot will guess the data.
Honk loudly, yank the handbrake, and restart Outlook.

questions.text: Business units feed internal company data into unauthorized free LLM tools without IT approval (Shadow IT). What immediate countermeasure applies?
questions.pictureDesc: Scenario: Blinding high beams from unauthorized leaks of company data into free LLMs.

answers.text: Provide a secure internal alternative (Microsoft 365 Copilot) and block unsafe AI URLs via Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps.
Draft in their slipstream and hijack their logins for private prompts.
Flash high beams at oncoming traffic to distract from the situation.
Ignore the problem as long as project deadlines are met.

questions.text: You join a one-hour Teams meeting 45 minutes late. How do you get up to speed quickly without disrupting others?
questions.pictureDesc: Scenario: You merge late into a running Teams meeting.

answers.text: Ask Copilot in chat: "Summarize the meeting so far and list open action items."
Loudly interrupt the speaker and demand a personal summary.
Pull onto the hard shoulder, mute your mic, and pretend the connection dropped.
Stay quiet and hope nobody asks you to contribute.

questions.text: After two weeks of vacation, 450 unread emails sit in your inbox. How does Copilot in Outlook clear the jam?
questions.pictureDesc: Scenario: Inbox jam. 450 unread messages block the road ahead.

answers.text: Use Summarize / Copilot in Outlook to quickly digest long email threads.
Mark all as read and hope nothing important was in there.
Reply "OK" to every email to fake activity.
Call in sick immediately. The issues will sort themselves out.

questions.text: You need to turn an existing Word concept into a PowerPoint deck for the client right away. How do you speed that up?
questions.pictureDesc: Scenario: Time pressure! A Word document must be transformed into PowerPoint slides fast.

answers.text: Open PowerPoint and tell Copilot: "Create a presentation based on this file [link]."
Manually paste the entire text onto one blank slide in 8 pt font.
Draw the slides with your finger on the fogged side window while driving.
Keep driving straight and ignore the meeting completely.

questions.text: You are furious about an email and want to type a harsh reply. How do you use Copilot in Outlook correctly?
questions.pictureDesc: Scenario: Elevated aggression risk in email traffic.

answers.text: Type a rough draft and have Copilot rewrite the tone to "Professional."
Have Copilot rewrite it with maximum exclamation points and ALL CAPS.
Slam the horn blindly and fire off the angry draft unread.
Find the sender and keep the wheel firm and straight.

questions.text: You want to ask Copilot questions about a Teams meeting, but recording and transcript were never started. What is the consequence?
questions.pictureDesc: Scenario: Poor visibility. Copilot lacks a data basis because nothing was recorded.

answers.text: Copilot has no data basis and cannot answer post-meeting questions.
Copilot invents a few plausible decisions to keep you happy.
Do nothing: no transcript means no action items.
Ask Copilot anyway and complain loudly about the weak answers.

questions.text: A Teams group chat has been arguing wildly for hours. You can't see the forest for the trees. How does Copilot help?
questions.pictureDesc: Scenario: Chaotic group chat with too many voices at once.

answers.text: Open Copilot in the chat and ask for the key decisions from the last 24 hours.
Manually scroll through 400 messages and write off the rest of the workday.
Leave the chat without a word and block everyone.
Flash the headlights in time with the Teams ringtone and let colleagues handle it.

questions.text: In a summary, Copilot suggests a project step that seems completely illogical. How do you respond?
questions.pictureDesc: Scenario: Possible optical illusion (hallucination) from the system.

answers.text: Critically check the cited sources ("citations"). AI can hallucinate.
Trust the AI blindly. If Copilot says the giraffe belongs there, it does.
Delete the whole document and start over on paper.
Accept the suggestion. You've seen far less logical decisions from humans.

questions.text: After three months of Copilot, the board questions the value because measurable revenue gains are still missing. How do you respond as a consultant?
questions.pictureDesc: Scenario: Executive checkpoint on the investment.

answers.text: Show realistic time-to-value horizons and define qualitative leading indicators (e.g., time saved on email) instead of purely monetary KPIs at this early stage.
Forge an Excel sheet claiming employees saved 400,000 hours.
Crank the bass and drown out the board's question with experimental dubstep. Strategic dominance.
Tamper with the speedometer so it looks like you're doing 200 mph.

questions.text: The company does not use Microsoft Purview sensitivity labels. Copilot gladly summarizes an unprotected salary document for every user. What is the mistake?
questions.pictureDesc: Scenario: An unprotected salary document sits exposed on the search index fast lane.

answers.text: Data classification was skipped before rollout. Missing Purview labels plus widespread "Anyone with the link" sharing give Copilot unrestricted access.
Copilot's curiosity setting is cranked too high.
Email everyone asking them please not to search for "salary table."
Immediately find that document via Copilot yourself. Strategic prep for the next salary review.

questions.text: Copilot stubbornly answers from hopelessly outdated HR policies on a SharePoint site abandoned for years. What is the fix?
questions.pictureDesc: Scenario: An outdated document blocks the proper flow of information.

answers.text: Establish a consistent information lifecycle: archive, delete, or exclude outdated sites from Microsoft Search.
Ban Copilot in the prompt from reading old stuff.
Tell employees to do the exact opposite of whatever Copilot says.
Propose updating current policies to match Copilot's answers.

questions.text: Usage metrics show 95% of users only use Copilot to summarize Teams meetings. Deeper use cases are completely missing. What do you do?
questions.pictureDesc: Scenario: One-sided vehicle use. Important assistance systems stay unused.

answers.text: Run targeted use-case workshops per department and build a Copilot Champions network.
Disable the summary feature to force users to get creative.
Ignore it. The licenses are already paid a year in advance anyway.
Propose holding all meetings in person from now on.

questions.text: A client asks: "Are my prompts used to train public AI models?" What is the correct answer in a corporate context?
questions.pictureDesc: Scenario: Confidential data-protection wall. No model training on customer data.

answers.text: No. With Microsoft 365 Copilot, training on customer data is contractually and technically excluded. Prompts, responses, and Graph data stay in your tenant.
Advise writing prompts as cryptic riddles so the model gets a migraine during training.
Yes, unless you prefix every request with "DO NOT SAVE THIS!!!" in ALL CAPS.
That can only be answered after a three-month steering committee and founding a Prompt Metadata Task Force.

questions.text: In a burst of enthusiasm the client bought 1,000 Copilot licenses but ignored onboarding. Only 50 are assigned. What is the consequence?
questions.pictureDesc: Scenario: 950 paid vehicles sit unused in the depot.

answers.text: Massive financial waste ("shelfware") that will wreck IT's business case at the next budget review.
The M365 system runs faster because 950 licenses idle quietly in the background.
Microsoft automatically cancels licenses after 30 days of inactivity.
Hand the remaining licenses to randomly selected employees.

questions.text: A heavily regulated enterprise client demands that Microsoft 365 Copilot must run on local servers in their own basement (on-prem) for privacy reasons. How do you respond?
questions.pictureDesc: Scenario: The client demands cloud operations from the basement district.

answers.text: Make it clear: Microsoft 365 Copilot is a pure cloud service. On-prem hosting is architecturally off the table.
Promise you can pull the Azure cloud onto a USB stick.
Officially move the client's corporate boundary into an Azure data center in Frankfurt.
Propose a very long LAN cable from the basement straight into the Azure data center.

questions.text: After rollout, employees have Copilot licenses but no idea how to write an effective prompt. Usage crashes. What is the next step?
questions.pictureDesc: Scenario: Employees are in the car but do not know how to steer.

answers.text: Provide a central prompt library and run department-specific prompt engineering training.
Threaten staff with termination unless they fire off at least five prompts a day.
Build an agent that unsolicited pings employees in Teams chat and nags them.
Keep driving. We planned to build agents for everything anyway.

questions.text: During a long chat, Copilot suddenly forgets instructions from the start of the conversation. Why?
questions.pictureDesc: Scenario: After a long drive, the navigation system forgets the starting point.

answers.text: The conversation blew past the session context window. Older context gets pushed out of the LLM window.
It lacks emotional drama. The prompt needs a line that if anything fails, the whole project team works weekends crying overtime.
The user typed a word with "X" in it. That clears the cache.
Someone forgot the magic "Make no mistakes!" as a secret system prompt.

questions.text: A European finance client wants to ensure no data leaves the EU for processing. Which Microsoft guarantee applies for Copilot?
questions.pictureDesc: Scenario: Regulatory border barrier for sensitive financial data.

answers.text: The EU Data Boundary: European customer data is stored and processed within the EU/EFTA.
None. Data is always routed by satellite via the US by default.
The admin must manually confirm every evening that the data stayed in Europe.
Recommend a VPN with servers in the EU.

questions.text: Copilot answers questions about current competitor market prices using internal three-year-old sales reports instead of web data. What is misconfigured?
questions.pictureDesc: Scenario: Outdated offline map instead of real-time traffic data.

answers.text: Web search (web grounding via Bing) is disabled in the admin center. Copilot only uses the internal Graph index.
The laptop gearbox has a mechanical fault in the AI module.
Point out that this is not an AI use case. A normal web search is enough.
Switch the radio to a news station and hope the AI mic picks up the market report.

questions.text: A managing director complains that Copilot in Outlook is not searching old emails. The mail sits in local .pst files on the hard drive. How do you fix it?
questions.pictureDesc: Scenario: Local luggage blocks access to the loading bay.

answers.text: Local .pst archives must be migrated into the Microsoft 365 online mailbox. Copilot only analyzes cloud-indexed data.
Pull the hard drive and mail it to Redmond so Microsoft can scan it.
Advise printing the emails and migrating them to the "German Cloud" of ring binders and a filing cabinet.
Get out, sit on the curb, and read the emails aloud so the phone mic records them.

questions.text: A user asks a complex multi-part question. The orchestration engine mixes up the processing order and returns a logically wrong result. How do you optimize that?
questions.pictureDesc: Scenario: Gearbox locks up when shifting too fast through the gears.

answers.text: Split complex prompts into logical sub-steps (prompt chaining) and use clear transition phrases in the user guidance.
Ask the user to resubmit the prompt until the desired result appears.
Ban the user from writing sentences with more than one comma.
Pretend you have an urgent call and end the conversation.

questions.text: Legal worries that Copilot will reproduce copyrighted text one-to-one when generating marketing copy. Which Microsoft guarantee protects the customer?
questions.pictureDesc: Scenario: Legal cover for possible rear-end collisions with protected material.

answers.text: The Customer Copyright Commitment, provided built-in content filters and guardrails were not deliberately bypassed.
Automatic deletion of all creative words from the M365 database.
Enterprise Data Protection.
There is no guarantee. Immediately revoke the marketing team's Copilot licenses.

questions.text: A user has used the same Copilot chat session for three weeks for every task. Copilot is sluggish and keeps mixing up topics. What is the cause?
questions.pictureDesc: Scenario: Three weeks of continuous driving without restarting the engine overloads the motor.

answers.text: The chat history token capacity is overloaded. For new topic areas you must start a new chat session to keep context clean.
Throw your phone out the driver window to reduce local network load.
Just reboot the computer.
By week 4, Copilot usually sorts itself out.

questions.text: Employees heavily use "Anyone with the link can edit" to share SharePoint files internally. Copilot finds those links and surfaces the data to colleagues who should never see it. How do you intervene?
questions.pictureDesc: Scenario: Open parking garage with no barrier. Anyone can take any car.

answers.text: Disable global "Anyone" sharing links in the SharePoint admin center and enforce named permissions.
Explain this is not a Copilot topic and flee the meeting.
Tell employees to only search for documents they are allowed to see.
Ask employees to email back any documents they found without authorization.

questions.text: A Copilot agent hits a knowledge base whose documents have not been maintained for years. Answers include outdated phone numbers and closed locations. What is the governance action?
questions.pictureDesc: Scenario: Misleading outdated road signs along the roadside.

answers.text: Introduce automated data lifecycle policies with expiry dates (TTL) and regular review loops for content owners.
Hire a student intern as human-in-the-loop to manually check the answers.
Simply reopen all old locations to preserve data consistency.
No action needed. Classic case of AI hallucination.

questions.text: After Copilot go-live there is a single optional 45-minute Teams training. Then the ACM project is declared "successfully completed." Three months later usage drops toward zero. What is the mistake?
questions.pictureDesc: Scenario: Driving lessons end after the first theory class. The car will not drive itself.

answers.text: A fundamental lack of continuous learning. Adoption is not a one-off event but an iterative process with feedback loops and ongoing use-case deep dives.
No mistake. The PowerPoint clearly says "successfully completed."
They should have extended the training to 46 minutes.
Panic-cite the Gartner Hype Cycle and declare you are already in the Trough of Disillusionment.

questions.text: The ACM team spreads adoption content only through a dusty intranet news page. Nothing reaches the business units. What structure is missing?
questions.pictureDesc: Scenario: Central radio station broadcasting without local antennas (champion network) in the valleys.

answers.text: An active champion network: local multipliers and key users in the departments who pass knowledge on organically, peer to peer, and in a practical way.
The company grapevine was underused as a communication channel.
The intranet page was not printed and handed out to employees.
A LinkedIn post marketing the whole thing as a theory exam for "Copilot Consultants" in driving-test style.
